steps.text: Determine whether the licensee’s fuel handling procedures are adequate to perform intended functions.
Determine whether fuel is moved and inspected consistent with the requirements of the TS and the licensee’s procedures.
Determine whether fuel handling activities are conducted in accordance with 10 CFR Part 20 and the licensee’s procedures and programs for radiation protection.
Determine whether the licensee satisfied security plan requirements for fuel movement activities.
Determine whether significant fuel movement or inspection problems are identified and resolved in accordance with the licensee’s procedural controls.
Determine whether the licensee was within TS limits and met procedural requirements before resuming normal operation after fuel movement.
Be aware of the facility’s plans and schedules for refueling or other major fuel movement.

Review the safety analysis report (SAR) or as-built facility descriptions, the TS, the licensee’s procedure(s), and the Security Plan for fuel design information, fuel movement methods, and requirements.
During the onsite inspection, be alert for significant differences in the SAR, or the TS descriptions of fuel and equipment as compared to the actual fuel and equipment.
Perform design change review and approval in accordance with 10 CFR 50.59 and the licensee’s procedures.
Verify if identified differences are reflected in the as-built description of the facility.
Observe one fuel movement activity.
Verify the final fuel location.
Review respective records.
Discuss with personnel involved in the most recent fuel movement activity.
Verify if the final location of the fuel is consistent with that specified in the licensee's records.
Conduct dry runs for operations of potential safety concern or of an unusual nature.
Discuss actions needed to avert or mitigate accidents during dry runs.
Handle fuel during refueling, fuel shuffling, and fuel inspection.
Transfer fuel into a core configuration, or to racks or containers designed to prevent accidental criticality.

Calculate the minimum number of fuel bundles for a critical mass with optimum moderation, separate the bundles by the use of fuel racks designed for this purpose, or remove only one bundle at a time to be inspected underwater and replaced before proceeding.
Monitor changes in the level of neutron flux.
Perform 1/M calculations independently by more than one person.
Inspect fuel and control rods in accordance with the TS and procedures.
Visually determine fuel integrity.
Check control rods to determine their capability to continue to function.
Investigate and resolve control rod failures.
Inspect the removed neutron source for leakage paths such as splitting or pitting.
Observe radiological controls during one fuel movement activity.
Review radiation protection records and discuss with responsible personnel for the most recent fuel movement activity.
Monitor or sample for airborne radioactive particulate matter.
Perform grab sampling in accordance with approved licensee procedures.
Perform grab sampling for airborne radioactive particulate matter in accordance with approved licensee procedures.
Monitor exits from controlled areas in accordance with 10 CFR Part 20 and licensee procedural requirements.
Use a Geiger Muller (GM) detector for exit surveys.
Shield the detector area or extend the controlled area to a low radiation area.
Perform surveys for contamination in accordance with licensee procedures.
Survey licensee personnel, tools, and equipment for contamination upon exit of the work area.
Check the work exit area for contamination when each person leaves.
Monitor radiation areas in accordance with Subpart F of 10 CFR Part 20, the TS, and the licensee's procedural requirements.
Survey periodically to determine whether actual radiation levels are consistent with area radiation monitors.
Check direct reading dosimeters frequently.
Wear personnel monitoring devices correctly and ensure they are appropriate for expected radiation fields and the type of work.
Observe security controls during one fuel movement activity.

Observe the resolution of one fuel movement problem, or if direct observation is not possible, identify problem areas by reviewing the licensee's logs and records and discussing with responsible personnel.
Verify that the licensee has resolved identified problems (e.g., fuel integrity) to meet TS requirements.
Observe one reactor startup after fuel movement, or if direct observation is not possible, review respective licensee logs and records and discuss with responsible personnel.

preconditions.text: Significant differences in the SAR, or the TS descriptions of fuel and equipment as compared to the actual fuel and equipment are identified.
If direct observation of a fuel movement activity is not possible at the time of inspection
Participants must be trained and understand their tasks.
If the licensee is changing the core configuration
If an installed neutron source is removed during refueling
Direct observation of radiological controls is not possible at the time of inspection.
Grab samples are used to substitute for continuous air monitors to verify compliance with 10 CFR Part 20 and the TS.
If area radiation levels are so high that a difference of about 0.2 millirem per hour (mr/hr) cannot be detected

constraints.text: Under no circumstances is the licensee to adjust schedules for these activities to fit the inspection schedule.
The use of approved procedures are required in accordance with TS and security plan requirements.
Safe fuel handling includes assurance that the fuel is not damaged, that accidental criticality does not occur, and that radiation and contamination hazards are minimized.
Failure of a fuel element can cause high air particulate alarm requiring evacuation, and substantial water/airborne radioactivity.
Fuel handling tools must be consistent with TS requirements and licensee procedures.
Handle fuel in accordance with the TS and applicable procedures.

Inadvertent criticality hazard: specific moderation, enrichment, poison, and core structure may allow much less than a cubic yard of typical fuel and water to have a critical mass (e.g., four bundles in a square matrix pose a hazard).
Some vane-type control blades might fall out of the core if broken.
A failure in one control rod could indicate that another might fail by the same mechanism.
Fuel movement can cause significant radiological hazards; a single fuel element can easily emit radiation at levels above 100 rem per hour at one foot.
Maintain occupational doses as low as reasonably achievable (ALARA).
An increase of 0.2 mr/hr generally represents contamination that would normally be considered gross contamination.
10 CFR Part 20, Subpart F requires surveys and ANSI/ANS 15.11, 1993 provides additional guidance in the section on 'Radioactive Contamination Monitoring.'
10 CFR Part 20, Subpart J provides the posting and labeling requirements for several radiation areas.
Personnel dosimetry is required by 10 CFR 20.1502 and is to be used as specified by the licensee's procedures.
New fuel is required to be kept in the security area and locked in a fuel vault or in some way secured.
New fuel is required to be kept in the security area and locked in a fuel vault or in some way secured so that it cannot be carried away by hand.
Spent fuel is normally controlled in storage racks in a spent fuel pool or in the reactor pool.
Changes to core configuration may require calibration of control rods, and determination of excess reactivity and shutdown margin.
